topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The worn vinyl of the old record player was still spinning, a ghost of the melody I’d poured my soul into. Then, a voice drifted through the open window—a rendition of "Starlight Echoes," my composition, played with a raw, aching vulnerability that was undeniably *mine*. My hands tightened around the lukewarm coffee mug until my knuckles were white. I froze, every muscle in my body locking up. I wanted to bolt, to disappear into the shadows of my cramped apartment, but I remained rooted, watching the silhouette of the stranger on the street, their fingers dancing across the fretboard as if they were breathing the notes I had created. A cold, heavy pressure settled in my chest, making it hard to draw a full breath. I slowly lowered the record, the silence rushing in to fill the void where that sound had just been, leaving behind a hollow, trembling ache.

[story 2]

I was cataloging old demos, a solitary, obsessive task, when the sound cut through the quiet of the studio. It was my track, the one I’d been agonizing over for months, being coaxed out of a guitar. The performer had an uncanny knack for capturing the nuance, the exact melancholic bend in the chorus that only I had conceived. A flush crept up my neck, hot and undeniable. I quickly minimized the playback, my fingers hovering over the keyboard as if to erase the evidence. Every instinct screamed at me to confront them, to demand an explanation, but a paralyzing wave washed over me. I just stared at the empty screen, my jaw tight, feeling a deep, internal churning—a frantic need to rewind time, to snatch the song back before it left my control and became something entirely separate.

[story 3]

The applause was warm, enveloping, and it was directed at someone else. As the final chord faded, the spotlight swung away, and I found myself standing backstage, the air thick with the scent of sweat and cheap whiskey. Then, I heard it—a faint echo of my melody, subtly altered, woven into the fabric of the other musician's improvisation. My stomach lurched. I felt a sudden, desperate urge to run toward the stage, to snatch the microphone, to stop the performance mid-flow. I kept my gaze fixed on the floor, focusing intensely on the scuff marks on the wooden floorboards. My breathing became shallow, rapid. It was as if a physical weight had been dropped onto my shoulders, an undeniable sense of trespass settling deep within my core, making every movement feel clumsy and exposed.
